countries: United States Minor Outlying Islands (Line Islands)
French Polynesia (Society Islands, Austral Islands, Rapa)
nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits clear-water coral reefs from shallow water to depths of at least about 30 m; generally observed grazing surface algal films and detritus from hard substrate of lagoon and seaward reef areas

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift detritus and graze surface algal films from live rock and other hard substrate. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis, brine, krill) in mixed-community feedings; vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

summary: The White Tail Bristletooth Tang is a small Central Pacific surgeonfish recognizable by its deep brown to purplish body and the abruptly white tail and caudal peduncle — the narrow section of the body just before the tail — from which its name derives. Generally considered reef-safe, it feeds primarily on algae and detritus.
careNotes:
The White Tail Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus flavicauda) is a small Central Pacific surgeonfish named for the abruptly white caudal peduncle and tail that contrasts sharply with its deep brown to purplish body. Adults generally reach around 14–17 cm in length.

A tank of roughly 400 L (about 105 gallons) with a long horizontal footprint is generally recommended. Bristletooth tangs are active grazers throughout the day and can become territorial toward similar species, so a spacious aquarium with ample open swimming lanes and extensive live rock provides the combination of grazing surface and physical territory that helps keep the fish settled.

Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges — a specific gravity of around 1.022–1.026, pH of 8.1–8.4, and alkalinity of 8–12 dKH.

Diet should lean heavily toward marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based herbivore foods offered at least three times per week are generally advised. Vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition in which pits or channels develop along the lateral line and around the head. This issue is widely reported in surgeonfish kept in poor water quality or on nutritionally deficient diets.

The White Tail Bristletooth Tang is generally considered reef-safe. Reports of coral nipping are uncommon and tend to involve individuals that are not being fed adequately. It is best kept as the only Ctenochaetus in the tank — aggression toward other bristletooth tangs and similarly shaped surgeonfish is commonly reported — though it tends to be peaceable with non-tang reef fish such as clownfish, gobies, blennies, and wrasses.

Like all surgeonfish, this species carries sharp retractable blades at the base of the tail — a feature of the family Acanthuridae that gives rise to the common name 'surgeonfish'. These blades are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on other fish during territorial disputes and on handlers during transfers; a soft net and care during handling are advisable.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for this species. Like most surgeonfish, Ctenochaetus flavicauda is presumed to spawn by releasing eggs and sperm into open water in the wild — a strategy known as pelagic broadcast spawning — with the resulting larvae drifting as plankton before settling. Spawning in a home aquarium is considered highly unlikely, as the species requires a level of space and natural pair-bonding cues that typical reef systems cannot provide.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for White Tail Bristletooth Tang. Provided max size (6.5 in / ~16.5 cm), tank minimum (70 gal / 265 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), family (Acanthuridae), and native range (Tahiti, South Pacific). Reached via the tangs-surgeons collection root supplied in the brief.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Ctenochaetus flavicauda Fowler, 1938), family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (16.0 cm TL), depth range (1–30 m), preferred temperature (24.4–28.8°C, mean 26.6°C), Central Pacific native range (US Minor Outlying Islands; Line, Society, Rapa and Austral Islands), habitat (coral reefs, shallow to 30 m), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and diagnostic feature (posterior caudal peduncle and fin abruptly white in life). Reef2Reef wiki had no species-specific page for C. flavicauda, so FishBase served as the first verified secondary.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Hobby retailer secondary used for tank-size triangulation. Confirmed max size (~6 in / 15 cm), minimum tank (90 gal / ~340 L, larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), semi-aggressive toward genus / peaceful with others, reef-safe, and Tahiti / South Pacific native range.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Third hobby source consulted for sizing and temperament triangulation. Stated max size 5–6 in (~13–15 cm), minimum 75 gal (~284 L), temperature 74–78°F (23–26°C), SG 1.024–1.026, generally peaceful but territorial toward other tangs, reef-safe via specialised teeth, and Central Pacific (Marshall Islands) native range.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records have been documented for this species. Like most surgeonfish, it is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild, releasing eggs and sperm into open water. Spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
